headline: Nasdaq Logs Longest Winning Streak Since 2021 as Investors Look Beyond War
summary: Oil prices have retreated.  The prospect of a peace deal that reopens the Strait of Hormuz has now powered the Nasdaq composite to its longest streak of daily gains since November 2021—a 10-session ascent that has lifted the tech-heavy index 14%.  The Dow Jones Industrial Average’s energy-hungry components have the blue-chip index lagging behind other major benchmarks, on Tuesday notching a 0.7% gain.

raw.summary: Oil prices have retreated.  The prospect of a peace deal that reopens the Strait of Hormuz has now powered the Nasdaq composite to its longest streak of daily gains since November 2021—a 10-session ascent that has lifted the tech-heavy index 14%.  The Dow Jones Industrial Average’s energy-hungry components have the blue-chip index lagging behind other major benchmarks, on Tuesday notching a 0.7% gain.

headline: Morgan Stanley and BofA Expected to Post Higher Profits After Big Banks’ Mixed Results
summary: Investors are set to hear from Bank of America and Morgan Stanley on Wednesday after a string of big banks’ first-quarter earnings reports reflected solid U.S. consumer health.  Results from JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, and Wells Fargo showed that measures of consumer credit, such as net charge-offs and delinquencies, were steady in the first quarter, even as borrowers faced higher energy prices prompted by the U.S.-Iran war.  Shares of Wells Fargo sunk 5.7% after the firm missed Wall Street's revenue and net interest income expectations, while JPMorgan's stock fell 0.8% and Citi's rose 2.6%.

raw.headline: Morgan Stanley and BofA Expected to Post Higher Profits After Big Banks’ Mixed Results

Results from JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, and Wells Fargo showed that measures of consumer credit, such as net charge-offs and delinquencies, were steady in the first quarter, even as borrowers faced higher energy prices prompted by the U.S.-Iran war.  Shares of Wells Fargo sunk 5.7% after the firm missed Wall Street's revenue and net interest income expectations, while JPMorgan's stock fell 0.8% and Citi's rose 2.6%.

That small move sits against a backdrop where some firms are trimming targets while others keep them higher, leaving you with a mixed but finely tuned view on how much upside remains. headline: Wall Street Traders Capitalize on Market Turbulence Tied to Iran War
summary: Middle East conflict and surging geopolitical tensions rattled markets in the first quarter.  JPMorgan Chase said Tuesday that its markets revenue jumped by 20% a year ago to $11.6 billion, a record, lifted by strong client activity across asset classes.  Volatile markets that prompt bank clients to reposition their portfolios and make big bets are typically boons for trading businesses.

Volatile markets that prompt bank clients to reposition their portfolios and make big bets are typically boons for trading businesses.

headline: Investment Banking Units Shine During Volatile Quarter
summary: Wall Street’s premier investment banking units put in solid performances during the first quarter, according to earnings reports from three of the nation’s largest banks on Tuesday.  JPMorgan Chase said that investment banking revenue rose 38% year over year to $3.1 billion for the quarter.  Citigroup said investment banking revenue increased 19% to $1.3 billion.

headline: Bank Earnings Show Consumer Resilience, Even as Pressures Mount
summary: When it comes to describing the U.S. economy, bank executives’ mot du jour is “resilient.”  “What we are seeing right now is that this is a resilient economy,” said Mike Santomassimo, Wells Fargo’s chief financial officer, on a call with reporters.  "The U.S. economy remained resilient in the quarter, with consumers still earning and spending and businesses still healthy,” JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon said in a statement.

headline: Banks see economy as 'resilient' but warn about rising energy prices
summary: The nation’s biggest banks posted another quarter of strong profits, helped by a resilient economy and a flurry of dealmaking for their investment banking units.  “There is an increasingly complex set of risks,” Jamie Dimon, CEO and chairman of JPMorgan Chase, said in a statement, referencing to wars, energy prices and trade wars as some of the current risks in the global economy.  This quarter, it was the investment banks at all of the major banks that drove revenue to Wall Street during the first three months of the year.

headline: Big Banks Offer New Details on Exposure to Private Credit
summary: Banks sought to reassure investors on Tuesday by giving more detail on their lending to private credit funds in their earnings reports, and described the steps they have taken to limit their risk exposure.  Banks, though, have argued that this lending is highly structured and protected by collateral that the banks pick themselves.  Within that category, about 23% of the exposure was to business-development companies, or BDCs.

headline: U.S. Economy Remains Resilient in Face of Iran War, Banks Say
summary: American households and businesses are holding up despite a turbulent start to the year that has threatened to roil the U.S. economy.  JPMorgan Chase Citigroup  and  Wells Fargo  said customers continued to spend, borrow and invest in the first quarter, even as a war erupted in the Middle East and worries mounted about risks from artificial intelligence, inflation and private credit.  JPMorgan Chief Executive  Jamie Dimon  warned that while the broader U.S. economy was largely in good shape, a “complex set of risks” threatened to derail its trajectory.

raw.headline: U.S. Economy Remains Resilient in Face of Iran War, Banks Say
raw.summary: American households and businesses are holding up despite a turbulent start to the year that has threatened to roil the U.S. economy.  JPMorgan Chase Citigroup  and  Wells Fargo  said customers continued to spend, borrow and invest in the first quarter, even as a war erupted in the Middle East and worries mounted about risks from artificial intelligence, inflation and private credit.  JPMorgan Chief Executive  Jamie Dimon  warned that while the broader U.S. economy was largely in good shape, a “complex set of risks” threatened to derail its trajectory.

headline: JPMorgan Traders Notch Record Revenue During Volatile Quarter
summary: A volatile first quarter for assets of all stripes—stocks, bonds, commodities—lifted JPMorgan’s trading business to a record.  The bank said its markets revenue jumped by 20% from a year ago to $11.6 billion, an all-time high.  JPMorgan’s markets business, housed in the firm’s commercial and investment bank, reported that fixed-income markets revenue rose by 21% to $7.1 billion.

headline: JPMorgan Reports Higher Profit and Revenue as Economy Chugs Along
summary: JPMorgan Chase  reported higher profit and revenue for the first quarter, with Wall Street operations booming and the U.S. economy remaining resilient.  Revenue rose 10% to $49.84 billion, compared with forecasts of $49.13 billion.  Chief Executive  Jamie Dimon  said that while the broader U.S. economy was largely in good shape, a “complex set of risks” including energy prices, geopolitical tensions, high fiscal deficits and elevated asset prices threatened to derail its trajectory.

summary: JPMorgan Chase reported a rise in first-quarter profit on Tuesday as its trading division got a boost from volatility across global markets. Net income rose to $16.5 billion, or $5.94...

headline: JPMorgan Deepens Blockchain Bet As Tokenization Meets Valuation Opportunity
summary: JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon is backing a stronger push into blockchain, tokenization, and crypto related activity. The bank is rebranding its Onyx platform, expanding the use of JPM Coin, and supporting a tokenized money market fund on Ethereum. These moves point to a clearer role for blockchain based products within JPMorgan Chase's broader digital strategy. For a bank of JPMorgan Chase's size and influence, a clearer embrace of blockchain and tokenization matters for how...
